fact: December 9 is exactly 343 days after January 1st - that's 7 cubed, making it a perfect cube number day!
In the Northern Hemisphere, squirrels are frantically burying their last acorns around this time, creating secret treasure maps in their tiny brains that they'll remember all winter long.
The sun sets so early in northern places like Alaska that by December 9, some towns only get about 4 hours of daylight - imagine eating lunch in the dark!
December 9 falls smack in the middle of Sagittarius season, when the archer constellation is shooting its arrow across the night sky.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, December 9 is when jacaranda trees explode with purple flowers, carpeting sidewalks in slippery purple petals.
Arctic foxes are switching from brown to pure white fur right around this date, like nature's own costume change for winter camouflage.
The moon on December 9 can create the most brilliant ice halos in cold climates - perfect rings of light that make the moon look like it's wearing a glowing crown.
December 9 is only 12 days before the winter solstice in the north, meaning each day loses about 30 seconds of sunlight - you can actually time it!
In many places, this is when lakes start making those creepy cracking and groaning sounds as ice forms - like the water is complaining about getting frozen.
Reindeer in the Arctic are growing their winter nose hairs extra thick by December 9, creating built-in air warmers that heat up freezing air before it hits their lungs.
The sunrise on December 9 in northern cities creates the most intense orange and pink colors because sunlight has to travel through way more atmosphere when the sun stays low.
December 9 is when many evergreen trees pump extra antifreeze chemicals into their needles, basically turning themselves into natural popsicles that won't freeze solid.
